rows.label: statement
rows.note: Note: Irregular verbs do not take '-ed'. Learn the past simple form of irregular verbs.

content.subtitle: Use
items: Single completed actions: Lisa joined a yoga class and discovered she loved it.
Habits in the past: Did you play football every Saturday when you were a child?
Permanent situations in the past: A well-known cyclist trained on the roads near our town.
General truths and facts about the past: Tennis didn't become an Olympic sport again until 1988.
The main events in a story: The striker received the ball, turned sharply, and fired it into the net.

content.subtitle: Emphatic form
content.description: We can also use did in past simple statements for emphasis.
examples: I did score that goal. Ask anyone who was there!

content.subtitle: Use
items: Actions happening at a particular moment in the past: At three in the afternoon, Marcus was practising his tennis serve.
Temporary situations in the past: Sophie was training at the national athletics centre that summer.
Annoying past habits (usually with always): When we trained together, my teammate was always arriving late for practice.
Actions in progress over a period of time: Oliver was cycling through the mountains all weekend.
Two actions in progress at the same time: Were the coaches watching our game while we were competing in the relay?
Background information in a story: The crowd was cheering loudly and the players were warming up on the pitch.

content.subtitle: Be careful
content.description: When one action in the past interrupts another action in progress, we use the past simple and the past continuous together. I was jogging in the park when I suddenly twisted my ankle.
notes: We do not use the past continuous for regular or repeated actions in the past. ✓ When we were at summer camp, we went swimming every morning.
We do not usually use stative verbs in continuous tenses.

content.subtitle: Use
items: Situations and states before the past: We'd been members of the cycling club for a few months before we entered our first race.
Completed actions before a moment in the past: I'd already reserved a spot at the climbing wall when they announced the session was free.
Completed actions where the important thing is the result at a moment in the past: We didn't feel like going for a run because we had just finished a tough football match.

content.subtitle: Notes
content.description: There is often little or no difference in meaning between the past perfect simple and the past simple. After we have used the past perfect simple once, we often then use the past simple instead.
items: Phrases such as It was the first/second/etc time ... are followed by the past perfect simple: It was the first time I'd taken part in a triathlon.

content.subtitle: Use
items: Actions continuing up to a moment in the past: By the time the coach called us in, we had been training on the court for two hours straight.
Actions stopping just before a moment in the past: Danny was out of breath because he had been sprinting around the athletics track.

content.subtitle: Use
items: Past habits, particularly for the distant past: When I was little, my dad would take me to the local leisure centre every Sunday.

content.subtitle: Be careful
content.description: We don't often use would in questions or negative statements with this meaning. In negative statements, we can use would never.

rows.label: negative
rows.left: didn't use to / never used to / used not to

content.subtitle: Use
items: Past habits and states, particularly for the distant past: My uncle used to go cycling every weekend before he moved abroad.

content.subtitle: Be careful
content.description: To talk about a past state, we can use used to, but not would.
notes: ✓ We used to have a sports club membership that included the swimming pool.

content.subtitle: Use
items: A situation that is familiar or no longer strange: I didn't enjoy early morning training at first, but now I'm used to it.
To talk about the process of becoming familiar with something, we use get used to: I'm gradually getting used to running longer distances each week.

content.subtitle: Be careful
content.description: Don't confuse **be/get used to + -ing** with **used to + infinitive**.
notes: ✓ I'm used to training before breakfast. (= It's no longer strange for me.)
✓ I used to train before breakfast. (= I did this in the past, but I don't anymore.)
